title: Overloading and local scope

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific source-linked draft lesson for Overloading and local scope; independent technical, source, pedagogy, accessibility, and runnable-example review remains required. sources.locator: Learn Java; accessed 2026-07; Classes and Objects, Arrays, and String processing learning paths; atomic topic: Overloading and local scope
sources.claim: Overload resolution selects an applicable declaration from compile-time argument types, while lexical scope determines which local, parameter, field, or type name a use denotes.

sources.claim: Overload resolution selects an applicable declaration from compile-time argument types, while lexical scope determines which local, parameter, field, or type name a use denotes.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Overload resolution selects an applicable declaration from compile-time argument types, while lexical scope determines which local, parameter, field, or type name a use denotes.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Calls to format(int) and format(double) select different overloads from argument types, while this.label distinguishes a field from a constructor parameter. Your goal is to resolve overload selection and repair shadowing or scope defects. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and branching; pf abstraction calls; Method contracts and parameters; Operators and conversions. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language-specific operational model
sections.body: Overload resolution selects an applicable declaration from compile-time argument types, while lexical scope determines which local, parameter, field, or type name a use denotes. This model is applied through the declared Java: Beginner to Intermediate behavior rather than a language-neutral slogan.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure model
sections.body: Return type alone cannot distinguish overloads, null can make unrelated reference overloads ambiguous, boxing and varargs affect applicability, and shadowing can hide fields. The learner must reject this documented misconception: Java chooses an overload from the runtime class of each argument and can use the expected return type to break every tie.

includes: Create unambiguous overloads and reason about local, parameter and class-level names.
Resolve overload selection and repair shadowing or scope defects.
excludes: Return type alone cannot distinguish overloads, null can make unrelated reference overloads ambiguous, boxing and varargs affect applicability, and shadowing can hide fields.

outcomes: Resolve overload selection and repair shadowing or scope defects.

examples.title: Overloading and local scope: direct language case
examples.situation: Calls to format(int) and format(double) select different overloads from argument types, while this.label distinguishes a field from a constructor parameter.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the concrete Java: Beginner to Intermediate state or tool result: Calls to format(int) and format(double) select different overloads from argument types, while this.label distinguishes a field from a constructor parameter.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Verify the result with this correction: Resolve overloads from compile-time argument types and applicable signatures; use distinct contracts and explicit qualification to avoid ambiguity and accidental shadowing.
examples.result: The example meets the atomic outcome because Resolve overloads from compile-time argument types and applicable signatures; use distinct contracts and explicit qualification to avoid ambiguity and accidental shadowing.

examples.title: Overloading and local scope: boundary and transfer case
examples.situation: Calling choose(null) is ambiguous between String and StringBuilder overloads, so the API is redesigned rather than relying on a surprising cast.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name what changed from the direct case: Calling choose(null) is ambiguous between String and StringBuilder overloads, so the API is redesigned rather than relying on a surprising cast.

Step 2 — Keep the language boundary explicit: Return type alone cannot distinguish overloads, null can make unrelated reference overloads ambiguous, boxing and varargs affect applicability, and shadowing can hide fields.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting but incorrect rule: Java chooses an overload from the runtime class of each argument and can use the expected return type to break every tie.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Resolve overload selection and repair shadowing or scope defects.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Java chooses an overload from the runtime class of each argument and can use the expected return type to break every tie.
misconceptions.correction: Resolve overloads from compile-time argument types and applicable signatures; use distinct contracts and explicit qualification to avoid ambiguity and accidental shadowing.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Predict both examples and identify the first observable contradiction in this belief: Java chooses an overload from the runtime class of each argument and can use the expected return type to break every tie.

analogy.example: a set of labeled workstations that accept copies of request values and process fixed trays or immutable text tickets can introduce Overloading and local scope, after the canonical Java: Beginner to Intermediate rule is stated first.
usefulFor: reasoning about method contracts, scope, arrays, and strings
limitations: reference values can still alias mutable objects and Unicode or overload rules have no exact workstation equivalent

recap.summary: You should now be able to resolve overload selection and repair shadowing or scope defects. Keep this boundary in view: Return type alone cannot distinguish overloads, null can make unrelated reference overloads ambiguous, boxing and varargs affect applicability, and shadowing can hide fields.
